EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense area of low pressure moved across the Tennessee valley and eventually up the east coast on December 18th and 19th. The initial precipitation began as a heavy wet snow on Friday afternoon December 18th. Accumulations around eastern Kentucky ranged between 1 and 4 inches. Ridgetop locations along the Virginia and west Virginia borders saw as much as 6 inches, with 13 inches reported on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County. This initial round of snow fell generally between 2 and 6 pm on the 18th. ||After several hours of nothing but rain, another round of widespread and intense snowfall began at around 10 pm. The Jackson weather office picked up 2.6 inches of snow between 10 pm and midnight on the 18th and there were 11 inches on the ground by early Saturday morning. By 7 am on Saturday December 19th 5 to 7 inches of snow blanketed the ground across much of southeast Kentucky. The highest snowfall totals for this event were 16 inches on Black Mountain, 13 inches on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County, and 11 inches at the Jackson weather office. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by the heavy wet snow with widespread and long lasting power outages reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pike county emergency management reported a power line that had been downed by heavy wet snow was on fire in some trees along River Road in Mouthcard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient associated with a deep area of low pressure that moved across the Great Lakes on December 9th, brought strong and gusty winds to eastern Kentucky. Widespread moderate to sometimes intense rain showers led to some flooding problems around the area as well. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received, inspite of the fact that the winds came up just short of warning criteria. A combination of wet ground and steep terrain allowed the sub warning criteria winds to bring down trees and power lines at many locations around eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the Barnes Mountain area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving up the east coast on December 5th brought subfreezing air and widespread light snowfall to eastern Kentucky. Most area received between 1 and 3 inches of the white stuff by Saturday afternoon, with locally higher amounts in the mountains along the Virginia and West Virginia borders.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and a half inches of snow fell 1 mile west of Van Lear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient associated with a deep area of low pressure that moved across the Great Lakes on December 9th, brought strong and gusty winds to eastern Kentucky. Widespread moderate to sometimes intense rain showers led to some flooding problems around the area as well. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received, inspite of the fact that the winds came up just short of warning criteria. A combination of wet ground and steep terrain allowed the sub warning criteria winds to bring down trees and power lines at many locations around eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down on Highway 460 East 6 miles southeast of Frenchburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense area of low pressure moved across the Tennessee valley and eventually up the east coast on December 18th and 19th. The initial precipitation began as a heavy wet snow on Friday afternoon December 18th. Accumulations around eastern Kentucky ranged between 1 and 4 inches. Ridgetop locations along the Virginia and west Virginia borders saw as much as 6 inches, with 13 inches reported on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County. This initial round of snow fell generally between 2 and 6 pm on the 18th. ||After several hours of nothing but rain, another round of widespread and intense snowfall began at around 10 pm. The Jackson weather office picked up 2.6 inches of snow between 10 pm and midnight on the 18th and there were 11 inches on the ground by early Saturday morning. By 7 am on Saturday December 19th 5 to 7 inches of snow blanketed the ground across much of southeast Kentucky. The highest snowfall totals for this event were 16 inches on Black Mountain, 13 inches on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County, and 11 inches at the Jackson weather office. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by the heavy wet snow with widespread and long lasting power outages reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow was on the ground. The total snowfall from 10 pm Friday night through early Saturday morning was 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coop weather observer measured 16 inches of snow on Black Mountain on December 22nd. The observer was not on site during the storm that dumped the snow, but did measure a snow depth of 18 inches of snow on Sunday, December 20th. Most or all of this snow accumulated on the mountain between Friday afternoon on December 18th and late in the day on Saturday, December 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coop weather observer measured 16 inches of snow on Black Mountain on December 22nd. The observer was not on site during the storm that dumped the snow, but did measure a snow depth of 18 inches of snow on Sunday, December 20th. Most or all of this snow accumulated on the mountain between Friday afternoon on December 18th and late in the day on Saturday, December 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere interacting with a potent cold front led to the formation of numerous strong to severe thunderstorms on Friday October 10th. Widespread wind damage was reported in several counties across eastern Kentucky. One tornado was also confirmed by an NWS assessment team in Estill County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief EF1 Tornado Touchdown confirmed by NWS Storm Survey Team about 5 miles south of Irvine on Murphys Ford Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong center of low pressure tracked out of the plains states to the Great Lakes region. Ahead of this low in the Ohio Valley, southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 and 60 mph were common throughout the day. These strong winds peaked in the early afternoon with the passage of a cold front, and diminished later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large signs were blown down in the city of Maysville.

This initial round of snow fell generally between 2 and 6 pm on the 18th. ||After several hours of nothing but rain, another round of widespread and intense snowfall began at around 10 pm. The Jackson weather office picked up 2.6 inches of snow between 10 pm and midnight on the 18th and there were 11 inches on the ground by early Saturday morning. By 7 am on Saturday December 19th 5 to 7 inches of snow blanketed the ground across much of southeast Kentucky. The highest snowfall totals for this event were 16 inches on Black Mountain, 13 inches on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County, and 11 inches at the Jackson weather office. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by the heavy wet snow with widespread and long lasting power outages reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured three quarters of an inch of snow 4 miles west of Paintsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong center of low pressure tracked out of the plains states to the Great Lakes region. Ahead of this low in the Ohio Valley, southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 and 60 mph were common throughout the day. These strong winds peaked in the early afternoon with the passage of a cold front, and diminished later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and large limbs were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense area of low pressure moved across the Tennessee valley and eventually up the east coast on December 18th and 19th. The initial precipitation began as a heavy wet snow on Friday afternoon December 18th. Accumulations around eastern Kentucky ranged between 1 and 4 inches. Ridgetop locations along the Virginia and west Virginia borders saw as much as 6 inches, with 13 inches reported on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County. This initial round of snow fell generally between 2 and 6 pm on the 18th. ||After several hours of nothing but rain, another round of widespread and intense snowfall began at around 10 pm. The Jackson weather office picked up 2.6 inches of snow between 10 pm and midnight on the 18th and there were 11 inches on the ground by early Saturday morning. By 7 am on Saturday December 19th 5 to 7 inches of snow blanketed the ground across much of southeast Kentucky. The highest snowfall totals for this event were 16 inches on Black Mountain, 13 inches on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County, and 11 inches at the Jackson weather office. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by the heavy wet snow with widespread and long lasting power outages reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow had fallen in southern Knox county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed across most of western Kentucky except the Henderson and Owensboro areas. Visibility was below one-quarter mile. There was a report of visibility only near 25 feet at Hopkinsville. Since temperatures were below freezing, light icing occurred in the thick fog. A light south wind flow of milder air over the snow and ice-covered ground set the stage for dense fog.

A weather disturbance extending from the Gulf Coast States to Kentucky brought a widespread snowfall to Eastern Kentucky beginning late in the afternoon of March 12th, lasting into the early morning hours of March 13th. Sleet was reported in many locations, along with some freezing rain. In general, one to four inches of snow fell across the area, with locally higher amounts where the heavier snow and sleet persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch of snow fell in North Corbin.

A strong low pressure center moved northeast from Oklahoma into south central Missouri by late afternoon, with a warm front extending east from the low across western Kentucky. The warm front served as a focusing mechanism for some of the ingredients needed for tornadogenesis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small rope tornado was observed by trained spotters. The tornado was seen occasionally lifting off the ground. No damage was located, possibly because it was located over fields. Maximum winds were likely around 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the region from January 27th through the 28th, producing snow, sleet, freezing rain, and plain rain across Eastern Kentucky. This occurred as a low pressure system moved northeast from the Gulf Coast states on the 27th, reaching Eastern Kentucky on the 28th. Runoff from the precipitation led to flooding of streams, creeks, and rivers, across portions of Eastern Kentucky. The flooding lingered in some areas through January 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded due to the ice and debris in the Red River causing it to overflow its banks. Roads closures included Pompeii, Adams Ridge, Rosslyn, Bowen and Maple Street. These roads were all in the Clay City and Stanton areas.

A major winter storm affected the region from January 27th through the 28th, producing snow, sleet, freezing rain, and plain rain across Eastern Kentucky. This occurred as a low pressure system moved northeast from the Gulf Coast states on the 27th, reaching Eastern Kentucky on the 28th. Runoff from the precipitation led to flooding of streams, creeks, and rivers, across portions of Eastern Kentucky. The flooding lingered in some areas through January 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow fell 1 mile east southeast of Moon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated high winds occurred in Harlan County on January 28th. These occurred as a major winter storm system was moving across Eastern Kentucky. The high winds caused a roof to be torn off a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust tore the roof off a trailer in the community of Dayhoit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms occurred in a brisk southerly wind flow ahead of a cold front over Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on U.S. Highway 41-A. The lightning damaged several appliances. A telephone line was shredded. The bolt exited the house on the back side, ripping pieces of vinyl siding at the exit point. The house sustained unspecified damage inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms occurred ahead of a cold front as it crossed into western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused considerable damage at the Oak Grove City Hall. Computer equipment used by the police department and city workers was damaged. Lightning struck the communications antenna on the side of the building. The main radio terminal used by emergency dispatchers was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms occurred in a brisk southerly wind flow ahead of a cold front over Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a house fire. The lightning struck through a television antenna. The antenna connected to the back of the house had scorch marks, and there were burns in wet grass around its base. The home sustained heavy smoke and fire damage. A pet cat died. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center moved northeast from Oklahoma into south central Missouri by late afternoon, with a warm front extending east from the low across western Kentucky. The warm front served as a focusing mechanism for some of the ingredients needed for tornadogenesis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two funnel clouds were reported on the south side of the community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed across most of western Kentucky except the Henderson and Owensboro areas. Visibility was below one-quarter mile. There was a report of visibility only near 25 feet at Hopkinsville. Since temperatures were below freezing, light icing occurred in the thick fog. A light south wind flow of milder air over the snow and ice-covered ground set the stage for dense fog.

A bow-shaped line of thunderstorms moved east across western Kentucky. The bowing line of thunderstorms occurred ahead of a strong mid-level low progressing eastward across southeast Kansas and northeast Oklahoma. Instability was limited north of a warm front over extreme southeast Missouri and western Tennessee, but the intensity of the approaching system and strong winds aloft contributed to locally damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The side of a storage building was torn off. A utility line was down.

Low pressure tracked east through the Ohio Valley during the afternoon. This set up a boundary extending from just west of Richmond, Indiana, through eastern Cincinnati, to Maysville, Kentucky. A mid level disturbance created a very strong source of localized lift, which produced snowfall rates exceeding two inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was measured in Brooksville.

A strong 500 mb shortwave trough and its associated 100-knot mid level jet streak tracked into the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon. A deepening surface low moved across central Illinois into Indiana, with a cold front trailing south-southwest into central Arkansas. Low level moisture increased in the warm sector, and along with surface heating,  mixed layer capes rose to near 1000 joules per kilogram. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front and tracked rapidly east-northeast.

A strong low pressure system tracked east to the Lower Ohio Valley, with a trailing cold front sweeping east across western Kentucky. Storms developed and increased along the advancing cold front as they moved into increasingly unstable air. Very strong low level wind shear was favorable for tornadoes. The storms became increasingly organized, with line segments and supercells moving at over 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported at the Dawson Springs exit on the Western Kentucky Parkway.

Storms developed and increased along the advancing cold front as they moved into increasingly unstable air. Very strong low level wind shear was favorable for tornadoes. The storms became increasingly organized, with line segments and supercells moving at over 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moving into the upper Midwest coupled with low pressure across Southeast caused severe thunderstorms to develop during the early morning hours of February 18th across south central Kentucky. A dry slot moved across the area during the morning hours and sufficient heating allowed the atmosphere to destabilize as a cold front moved across central Illinois into Indiana and Kentucky. Thunderstorms redeveloped during the late morning and afternoon hours. Severe hail was the biggest threat with these storms despite a few tornado warnings being issued. Areas most effected were across south central Kentucky near the Kentucky and Tennessee border with a few reports just south of Lexington. Hail size reports ranged from penny size to golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across Eastern Kentucky on February 3rd, bringing snow showers to the area. Winds shifted to the northwest as the low moved east of the area. The northwest winds, combined with a moisture connection off Lake Michigan, resulted in additional snow showers late on February 3rd, continuing into the 4th. A widespread snowfall of 3 to 6 inches fell over much of the area, with locally higher amounts. Areas south of a line from Mount Vernon to Manchester saw lesser amounts of snow, with 1 to 3 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and four tenths of an inch of snow fell in Stanton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from the Southern Plains to the Lower Great Lakes region on February 11th. An associated cold front also moved through Eastern Kentucky during the afternoon of the 11th. Damaging non-thunderstorm winds occurred both ahead of and behind the front. Along the front, a line of showers and thunderstorms brought more damaging winds to the area. Thousands of people were without power, some for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a barn by thunderstorm wind gusts.

Along the front, a line of showers and thunderstorms brought more damaging winds to the area. Thousands of people were without power, some for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted and a roof was damaged on the Hal Rogers Parkway near mile marker 38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moving into the upper Midwest coupled with low pressure across Southeast caused severe thunderstorms to develop during the early morning hours of February 18th across south central Kentucky. A dry slot moved across the area during the morning hours and sufficient heating allowed the atmosphere to destabilize as a cold front moved across central Illinois into Indiana and Kentucky. Thunderstorms redeveloped during the late morning and afternoon hours. Severe hail was the biggest threat with these storms despite a few tornado warnings being issued. Areas most effected were across south central Kentucky near the Kentucky and Tennessee border with a few reports just south of Lexington. Hail size reports ranged from penny size to golf ball size.

A low pressure system moved across Eastern Kentucky on February 3rd, bringing snow showers to the area. Winds shifted to the northwest as the low moved east of the area. The northwest winds, combined with a moisture connection off Lake Michigan, resulted in additional snow showers late on February 3rd, continuing into the 4th. A widespread snowfall of 3 to 6 inches fell over much of the area, with locally higher amounts. Areas south of a line from Mount Vernon to Manchester saw lesser amounts of snow, with 1 to 3 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow fell in Inez.

Widespread dense fog developed across most of western Kentucky except the Henderson and Owensboro areas. Visibility was below one-quarter mile. There was a report of visibility only near 25 feet at Hopkinsville. Since temperatures were below freezing, light icing occurred in the thick fog. A light south wind flow of milder air over the snow and ice-covered ground set the stage for dense fog.

A bow-shaped line of thunderstorms moved east across western Kentucky. The bowing line of thunderstorms occurred ahead of a strong mid-level low progressing eastward across southeast Kansas and northeast Oklahoma. Instability was limited north of a warm front over extreme southeast Missouri and western Tennessee, but the intensity of the approaching system and strong winds aloft contributed to locally damaging winds and hail.

A strong low pressure system tracked east to the Lower Ohio Valley, with a trailing cold front sweeping east across western Kentucky. Storms developed and increased along the advancing cold front as they moved into increasingly unstable air. Very strong low level wind shear was favorable for tornadoes. The storms became increasingly organized, with line segments and supercells moving at over 45 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued into Hopkins County from Christian County for about a mile. The tornado deposited insulation in tree tops from a house in Christian County. Little additional damage occurred after crossing the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong 500 mb shortwave trough and its associated 100-knot mid level jet streak tracked into the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon. A deepening surface low moved across central Illinois into Indiana, with a cold front trailing south-southwest into central Arkansas. Low level moisture increased in the warm sector, and along with surface heating,  mixed layer capes rose to near 1000 joules per kilogram. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front and tracked rapidly east-northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house, damaging electrical devices.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures during the January 27 ice storm were warm enough over the Green River basin to allow for rapid runoff. The Green River rose above flood stage at Paradise on the 29th and continued rising into early February. Through the end of January, only minor flooding of fields occurred near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a northwestward arcing outflow boundary from near Nashville, TN towards Evansville, IN. The atmosphere immediately to the west of this boundary became very unstable as strong afternoon heating pushed temperatures into the lower to mid 90's. As the storms organized into a well-defined complex, they accelerated toward the south-southeast. The accelerating storm complex produced locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported street signs were blown down and tree debris was in roadways. Winds were estimated near 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least a couple of small to medium size trees were blown down, including one that was across a road. A metal roof was blown off a garage east of Bremen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure built north from the Lower Mississippi Valley, keeping a hot and humid air mass over northwest Kentucky. The afternoon heat index peaked near 105 degrees. Actual air temperatures reached the lower to mid 90's.
A few severe thunderstorms developed over northeast Kentucky during the afternoon and evening as a cold front moved through the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed over northeast Kentucky during the afternoon and evening as a cold front moved through the Ohio Valley.
Supercells developed across northeast Kentucky during the evening as a warm front moved across the region, producing large hail and two tornadoes.

Thunderstorms rapidly developed along a surface frontal boundary from central Illinois into southern Missouri. Extreme instability and favorable wind shear ahead of the activity promoted organized and isolated supercell storms. As the storms crossed the Ohio River into western Kentucky, they weakened quickly, but not before producing a microburst near Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated observing system at Barkley Regional Airport measured a wind gust to 64 mph. A few large tree limbs were blown down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of thunderstorms increased in intensity as it moved east across western Kentucky into an increasingly unstable atmosphere. Damaging surface winds were enhanced by a strong 50-knot rear-inflow jet feeding into the complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the 911 dispatch center, knocking out the dispatch consoles and phone lines. Lightning was blamed for a nearby garage fire that occurred about the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a surface front extending westward from central Kentucky into far southern Illinois. The air mass became very unstable in this region, where mixed layer CAPES were from 3000 to 4000. Strengthening deep layer wind shear supported organized severe storms as the activity spread east-southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crops were badly damaged by hail. A field of young corn was beaten and shredded by the hail, and a wheat crop was damaged. Other damaged crops included tobacco, vegetables, and fruit.

Actual air temperatures reached the mid to upper 90's both days.

Thunderstorms quickly formed across the Lower Wabash Valley and northwest Kentucky in the vicinity of an eastward moving upper level disturbance. The boundary layer air mass in this region was characterized by temperatures in the lower 80's and dew points in the lower 70's. The combination of a moist low level environment, slightly backed wind flow along a northwest to southeast oriented warm front, and at least 40 knots of deep westerly shear promoted semi-discrete supercells with large hail, damaging winds, and isolated tornadoes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 62 mph was measured at the Henderson City-County Airport. A hangar door was blown off.

An upper level shortwave rotating southeast around an upper level low over the Great Lakes enhanced storm development. Deep layer wind shear was unseasonably strong due to the proximity of the deep upper low.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a surface front extending westward from central Kentucky into far southern Illinois. The air mass became very unstable in this region, where mixed layer CAPES were from 3000 to 4000. Strengthening deep layer wind shear supported organized severe storms as the activity spread east-southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of thunderstorms increased in intensity as it moved east across western Kentucky into an increasingly unstable atmosphere. Damaging surface winds were enhanced by a strong 50-knot rear-inflow jet feeding into the complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along a rural road, not far from the intersection of U.S. Highways 62 and 641. Three barns or farm outbuildings were nearly destroyed. Four 1500-pound hay bales were tossed end over end. Metal debris was thrown into trees at a 90-degree angle to the left of the path. Large hardwood trees and other trees were uprooted. Small limbs were broken out of some trees halfway up. A power pole was pushed over. An eyewitness reported a sudden increase in whirling debris. Peak winds were estimated near 105 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty meteorologist estimated winds gusted to 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds occurred in the Oak Grove area, southwest of Hopkinsville. A wind gust to 63 mph was measured at the junction of Interstate 24 and Highway 107 southwest of Hopkinsville. Numerous trees were down, blocking roads. Shingles were off some roofs. Interstate signs were blown down, partially blocking the intersection of Interstate 24 and Highway 41A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms quickly formed across the Lower Wabash Valley and northwest Kentucky in the vicinity of an eastward moving upper level disturbance. The boundary layer air mass in this region was characterized by temperatures in the lower 80's and dew points in the lower 70's. The combination of a moist low level environment, slightly backed wind flow along a northwest to southeast oriented warm front, and at least 40 knots of deep westerly shear promoted semi-discrete supercells with large hail, damaging winds, and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported east of the Owensboro-Daviess County airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms quickly formed across the Lower Wabash Valley and northwest Kentucky in the vicinity of an eastward moving upper level disturbance. The boundary layer air mass in this region was characterized by temperatures in the lower 80's and dew points in the lower 70's. The combination of a moist low level environment, slightly backed wind flow along a northwest to southeast oriented warm front, and at least 40 knots of deep westerly shear promoted semi-discrete supercells with large hail, damaging winds, and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of very large hail crossed the city of Owensboro. Hail was reported up to quarter size on the west side of the city, up to golf ball size downtown, and baseball size on the east side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western Kentucky was located in the destabilizing warm sector of a surface low across northern Missouri. Mixed layer CAPE values at or above 1000 j/kg aided thunderstorm intensification as large scale lift was maintained ahead of the surface low.  Deep-layer wind shear on the order of 40 to 50 knots contributed to persistent rotating updrafts. A few tornadoes occurred with rotating storms that crossed a warm front draped across southern parts of western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down on Highway 164 on the northwest edge of the Fort Campbell military reservation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient associated with a deep area of low pressure that moved across the Great Lakes on December 9th, brought strong and gusty winds to eastern Kentucky. Widespread moderate to sometimes intense rain showers led to some flooding problems around the area as well. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received, inspite of the fact that the winds came up just short of warning criteria. A combination of wet ground and steep terrain allowed the sub warning criteria winds to bring down trees and power lines at many locations around eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pine tree was blown down in Pikeville, doing minor damage to a home and fence nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense area of low pressure moved across the Tennessee valley and eventually up the east coast on December 18th and 19th. The initial precipitation began as a heavy wet snow on Friday afternoon December 18th. Accumulations around eastern Kentucky ranged between 1 and 4 inches. Ridgetop locations along the Virginia and west Virginia borders saw as much as 6 inches, with 13 inches reported on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County. This initial round of snow fell generally between 2 and 6 pm on the 18th. ||After several hours of nothing but rain, another round of widespread and intense snowfall began at around 10 pm. The Jackson weather office picked up 2.6 inches of snow between 10 pm and midnight on the 18th and there were 11 inches on the ground by early Saturday morning. By 7 am on Saturday December 19th 5 to 7 inches of snow blanketed the ground across much of southeast Kentucky. The highest snowfall totals for this event were 16 inches on Black Mountain, 13 inches on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County, and 11 inches at the Jackson weather office. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by the heavy wet snow with widespread and long lasting power outages reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and a half to 2 inches of snow had fallen 4 miles west of Prestonsburg since around 10 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient associated with a deep area of low pressure that moved across the Great Lakes on December 9th, brought strong and gusty winds to eastern Kentucky. Widespread moderate to sometimes intense rain showers led to some flooding problems around the area as well. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received, inspite of the fact that the winds came up just short of warning criteria. A combination of wet ground and steep terrain allowed the sub warning criteria winds to bring down trees and power lines at many locations around eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down onto a home in Virgie, causing the home to catch fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of strong thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across portions of Pulaski, Rockcastle, Perry, Wayne, Jackson, Whitley, and Owsley counties during the early morning hours of September 26th. Twenty four hour rainfall totals through 7 am on September 26th ranged from 1.01 inches, as reported by the Beattyville cooperative weather observer, to 3.48 inches as reported by the iflows network in Buffalo, KY in Owsley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water was flowing over the road at the KY 80 and US 27 intersection. The water was still over the road at that location but was receding as of 9:35 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Ohio River Valley caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Smithland gage, where flood stage is 40 feet, the river crested at 41.33 feet on the 12th. Agricultural bottomland and low-lying areas were flooded. A private campground at Birdsville was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged ice and snow storm began around just after midnight Tuesday January 27 and continued through 11 am Wednesday January 28. The mixed freezing rain and snow that fell over central Kentucky was just one aspect of a widespread overrunning event that brought destructive winter weather from Texas, through the Ohio Valley, into New England. Arctic air centered across the Upper Midwest supplied low level cold air. Several disturbances moved east across a nearly stationary front stalled across the Tennessee Valley.||Snow, mixing with sleet, brought accumulations of around 2 to 4 inches from areas just south of the Ohio River through Frankfort and the northern Bluegrass Region by dawn on January 27th. Farther south during this time, ice accumulations ranging from one half to just over one inch stretched from Ohio county eastward through the southern Bluegrass Region. Along the Tennessee border, ice on elevated surfaces averaged one quarter of an inch. ||During the afternoon of the 27th and into the morning hours on the 28th, freezing rain developed along and north of interstate 64 and brought widespread ice accumulations averaging 1/2 inch. Rain fell over south central Kentucky. Icing lead to widespread tree damage and power outages by the morning of the 28th. Colder air arrived after dawn on the 28th. Freezing rain changed back to snow with additional accumulations of 1 to 3 inches along and north of Interstate 64.||This winter storm brought the most widespread damage due to icing in recent memory across Kentucky. Ice accumulated over 1 inch across central portions of the Commonwealth, including Grayson, Ohio, Butler and Breckinridge counties. Over 600,000 residences lost power, exceeding the number of outages due to the remnants of hurricane Ike last September. Utility companies stated that this event brought the greatest number of outages from any weather event in history. ||The almost continuous rain or snow over a 36 hour period brought 3 to 4 inches of precipitation over a wide area and even lead to some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain through mid morning on Tuesday January 27th brought ice accretions of 1/4 to 1/2 inch. Rain changed to snow after dawn Wednesday. Another inch of snow accumulated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet, mixing with snow at times, brought 2 to 4 inches from south to north across the Louisville metro area through 8 am Tuesday, February 27th. Freezing rain beginning later that afternoon brought an additional 1/2 inch of ice accretion. Freezing rain changed to snow after dawn Wednesday. An additional 2 to 3 inches of snow fell. The icing led to widespread power outages across the Louisville metro area. An estimated 230,000 home and businesses lost power. Many residents stayed without power for up to one week. A portable generator lead to carbon monoxide poisoning that killed 3 persons in a Louisville home Thursday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of strong thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across portions of Pulaski, Rockcastle, Perry, Wayne, Jackson, Whitley, and Owsley counties during the early morning hours of September 26th. Twenty four hour rainfall totals through 7 am on September 26th ranged from 1.01 inches, as reported by the Beattyville cooperative weather observer, to 3.48 inches as reported by the iflows network in Buffalo, KY in Owsley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Someone from the Park and Forest Service measured a rainfall amount of 1.84 inches between 6 and 7 am near Somerset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms became stationary from west to east across the southern portion of western Kentucky. The storms were fed by a rather strong west-southwest low level jet up to 40 knots. A surface cold front became stationary across western Kentucky, providing a focus for the storms. Steering winds aloft were nearly parallel to the front. Excessive rainfall occurred in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 24-hour rainfall total ending at 7 A.M. May 9th was 1.89 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Mississippi River Valley caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Mississippi River occurred for most of the month of May. At the New Madrid, Missouri gage, where flood stage is 34 feet, the river crested at 39.11 feet on the 15th. Agricultural bottomland and low-lying areas were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Green River occurred. At the Calhoun Lock and Dam gage, where flood stage is 23 feet, the river crested at 24.51 feet on the 13th. Agricultural bottomland and low-lying areas were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of strong thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across portions of Pulaski, Rockcastle, Perry, Wayne, Jackson, Whitley, and Owsley counties during the early morning hours of September 26th. Twenty four hour rainfall totals through 7 am on September 26th ranged from 1.01 inches, as reported by the Beattyville cooperative weather observer, to 3.48 inches as reported by the iflows network in Buffalo, KY in Owsley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that water had backed up over Highway 174 in the Haldeman and Hays Crossing areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms became stationary from west to east across the southern portion of western Kentucky. The storms were fed by a rather strong west-southwest low level jet up to 40 knots. A surface cold front became stationary across western Kentucky, providing a focus for the storms. Steering winds aloft were nearly parallel to the front. Excessive rainfall occurred in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clarks River flooded after a series of heavy downpours on the 8th and 9th. A young boy who was allowed to play in Clarks River floodwaters was rescued unhurt. The boy drifted about 1,500 feet before he was pulled into a rescue boat.

The mixed freezing rain and snow that fell over central Kentucky was just one aspect of a widespread overrunning event that brought destructive winter weather from Texas, through the Ohio Valley, into New England. Arctic air centered across the Upper Midwest supplied low level cold air. Several disturbances moved east across a nearly stationary front stalled across the Tennessee Valley.||Snow, mixing with sleet, brought accumulations of around 2 to 4 inches from areas just south of the Ohio River through Frankfort and the northern Bluegrass Region by dawn on January 27th. Freezing rain developed later that afternoon and continued into the early morning hours on Wednesday. One quarter to one half inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces. Rain changed to snow after dawn Wednesday and brought another 2 inches by noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense area of low pressure moved across the Tennessee valley and eventually up the east coast on December 18th and 19th. The initial precipitation began as a heavy wet snow on Friday afternoon December 18th. Accumulations around eastern Kentucky ranged between 1 and 4 inches. Ridgetop locations along the Virginia and west Virginia borders saw as much as 6 inches, with 13 inches reported on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County. This initial round of snow fell generally between 2 and 6 pm on the 18th. ||After several hours of nothing but rain, another round of widespread and intense snowfall began at around 10 pm. The Jackson weather office picked up 2.6 inches of snow between 10 pm and midnight on the 18th and there were 11 inches on the ground by early Saturday morning. By 7 am on Saturday December 19th 5 to 7 inches of snow blanketed the ground across much of southeast Kentucky. The highest snowfall totals for this event were 16 inches on Black Mountain, 13 inches on Flatwood Mountain in Pike County, and 11 inches at the Jackson weather office. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by the heavy wet snow with widespread and long lasting power outages reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported a measured snowfall of 3.6 inches since midnight. The storm total snowfall west of Prestonsburg was 5.5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow had fallen at the Williamsburg Airport with 3 inches on the ground in Williamsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient associated with a deep area of low pressure that moved across the Great Lakes on December 9th, brought strong and gusty winds to eastern Kentucky. Widespread moderate to sometimes intense rain showers led to some flooding problems around the area as well. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received, inspite of the fact that the winds came up just short of warning criteria. A combination of wet ground and steep terrain allowed the sub warning criteria winds to bring down trees and power lines at many locations around eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across Highway 3257 in northern McCreary county. Trees were also blown down across KY741 near Stearns.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Harvest Ridge Road, Tolson Drive and Sandy Ridge Road near Campton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast Kentucky was on the northwestern fringe of the heavy snow associated with a storm that moved out of the eastern Gulf of Mexico and across southern Georgia late Friday, the 18th.  The storm then moved off the North Carolina coast early on the 19th.||The precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of the 18th, then switched over to heavy wet snow during the evening hours.  While Lawrence County received heavy snow, lighter amounts of only 2 to 3 inches were common across Carter, Greenup and Boyd Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The total storm snow accumulation was mostly in the 5 to 7 inch range across Lawrence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of strong thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding across portions of Pulaski, Rockcastle, Perry, Wayne, Jackson, Whitley, and Owsley counties during the early morning hours of September 26th. Twenty four hour rainfall totals through 7 am on September 26th ranged from 1.01 inches, as reported by the Beattyville cooperative weather observer, to 3.48 inches as reported by the iflows network in Buffalo, KY in Owsley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water was flowing into one lane of Highway 39 in the Nelson Valley area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. Low pressure tracked up the east coast, with the westward extent of its moisture reaching into Ohio and northern Kentucky. As the upper low crossed, temperatures cooled and rain changed to snow, accumulating to 1 to 3 inches for much of northern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county sheriff in Vanceburg measured 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. Low pressure tracked up the east coast, with the westward extent of its moisture reaching into Ohio and northern Kentucky. As the upper low crossed, temperatures cooled and rain changed to snow, accumulating to 1 to 3 inches for much of northern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county sheriff in Maysville measured a half inch of snow in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light accumulations of ice formed on elevated surfaces, including vehicles and highway overpasses. Most of the icing problems were in the Ohio River counties from Owensboro to Paducah and Ballard County. Up to one-eighth inch of ice was on vehicle windshields. The Interstate 24 bridge over the Ohio River was closed for a short time due to several accidents on the bridge. A few icy spots were observed on overpasses in the Owensboro area. A low pressure system skirted just southeast of the region, bringing a wintry mix of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient associated with a deep area of low pressure that moved across the Great Lakes on December 9th, brought strong and gusty winds to eastern Kentucky. Widespread moderate to sometimes intense rain showers led to some flooding problems around the area as well. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received, inspite of the fact that the winds came up just short of warning criteria. A combination of wet ground and steep terrain allowed the sub warning criteria winds to bring down trees and power lines at many locations around eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2.5 to 3 foot diameter tree was fell on a pregnant woman and struck her in the head. She was taken to a local hospital and pronounced D.O.A. Attempts to save her baby were also unsuccessful. A young male involved in the same incident was also injured and was released from the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the month, several storm systems raced east to northeast across the region. Between 1 and 3 inches of rain were common during the first nine days of the month. As a result, a number of rivers approached or exceeded flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Green River occurred. At the Paradise gage, the river crested at 382.08 feet on the 12th. Flood stage there is 380 feet. Minor flooding of low-lying fields and pastureland occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved slowly southeast through the early morning hours. These storms moved through a very moist and unstable atmosphere. Small-scale bow echoes produced locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust to 68 mph was measured on Highway 136.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Ohio River Valley caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Paducah gage, where flood stage is 39 feet, the river crested at 42.83 feet on the 12th. Agricultural bottomland and low-lying areas were flooded. Portions of the riverfront park near downtown Paducah were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a very unstable atmosphere in the heat of the afternoon. Remnant upper level vorticity centers originating from convective complexes over Arkansas and Missouri helped generate these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The quarter-size hail occurred at the interchange of Interstate 24 and Highway 293.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved up the Ohio River Valley and overspread northeast Kentucky during the predawn hours of Tuesday the 27th.  The snow quickly accumulated 2 to 5 inches by late morning. |   |By afternoon, the snow had decreased and transitioned to intermittent light freezing rain.  Temperatures were in the mid and upper 20s.  The intensity of the freezing rain increased for the evening hours, with temperatures still in the upper 20s.  Ice accretions of around a half inch were widespread.  The intensity of the freezing rain decreased during the predawn hours of the 28th, while temperature crept up toward the freezing mark.  Once the low pressure center lifted northeast through West Virginia during the daylight hours, the storm ended as a quick burst of snow.  This added a coating of snow on top of the ice.||Numerous trees fell or sagged onto power lines. Evergreen trees were especially vulnerable.  The trees were not only coated with ice, but the rain added weight to the snow that was already on the trees.  Many roads were impassable due to downed trees and power lines.  Only a few homes sustained damaged from the fallen trees. ||Kentucky Power reported nearly 18 thousand customers without electricity.  Many rural areas were without electricity for 3 to 4 days.  Shelters were open for those residents without auxilary heat.         ||These 4 northeast Kentucky counties were included in the major disaster declaration by the President.  See FEMA disaster number 1818 for more details.

A deepening low pressure system lifted northeast across northern Illinois into Lower Michigan. On the back side of the low, strong west winds gusted to around 45 mph in most places. The peak wind gusts in western Kentucky were 53 mph at a mesonet site near Cadiz (Trigg County) and at a spotter's location on the west side of Owensboro. A four-inch diameter tree branch was blown down at the spotter's location on the west side of Owensboro. A tree was blown down across U.S. Highway 41 between Henderson and the Ohio River. The southbound lanes were blocked.

A four-inch diameter tree branch was blown down at the spotter's location on the west side of Owensboro. A tree was blown down across U.S. Highway 41 between Henderson and the Ohio River. The southbound lanes were blocked.

A deepening low pressure center tracked north-northeast from northeast Texas to eastern Missouri, then eventually into Iowa. Strong southeast winds gradually turned into the southwest. Peak gusts were generally from 40 to 48 mph across western Kentucky. A trained spotter in Owensboro measured a peak gust of 48 mph. An off-duty meteorologist at Calvert City in northern Marshall County measured a gust to 46 mph. In Paducah, an 85-foot tall radio communication tower on top of a hospital broke off about 17 feet up from the base. The tower landed across the hospital roof. Scattered power outages were reported due to weakened tree limbs falling on power lines.

An off-duty meteorologist at Calvert City in northern Marshall County measured a gust to 46 mph. In Paducah, an 85-foot tall radio communication tower on top of a hospital broke off about 17 feet up from the base. The tower landed across the hospital roof. Scattered power outages were reported due to weakened tree limbs falling on power lines.

A deepening low pressure system lifted northeast across northern Illinois into Lower Michigan. On the back side of the low, strong west winds gusted to around 45 mph in most places. The peak wind gusts in western Kentucky were 53 mph at a mesonet site near Cadiz (Trigg County) and at a spotter's location on the west side of Owensboro. A four-inch diameter tree branch was blown down at the spotter's location on the west side of Owensboro. A tree was blown down across U.S. Highway 41 between Henderson and the Ohio River. The southbound lanes were blocked.

Snow moved up the Ohio River Valley and overspread northeast Kentucky during the predawn hours of Tuesday the 27th.  The snow quickly accumulated 2 to 5 inches by late morning. |   |By afternoon, the snow had decreased and transitioned to intermittent light freezing rain.  Temperatures were in the mid and upper 20s.  The intensity of the freezing rain increased for the evening hours, with temperatures still in the upper 20s.  Ice accretions of around a half inch were widespread.  The intensity of the freezing rain decreased during the predawn hours of the 28th, while temperature crept up toward the freezing mark.  Once the low pressure center lifted northeast through West Virginia during the daylight hours, the storm ended as a quick burst of snow.  This added a coating of snow on top of the ice.||Numerous trees fell or sagged onto power lines. Evergreen trees were especially vulnerable.  The trees were not only coated with ice, but the rain added weight to the snow that was already on the trees.  Many roads were impassable due to downed trees and power lines.  Only a few homes sustained damaged from the fallen trees. ||Kentucky Power reported nearly 18 thousand customers without electricity.  Many rural areas were without electricity for 3 to 4 days.  Shelters were open for those residents without auxilary heat.         ||These 4 northeast Kentucky counties were included in the major disaster declaration by the President.  See FEMA disaster number 1818 for more details.

With cold air in place and sufficient moisture content in the lowest levels of the atmosphere, freezing fog developed along the Ohio River near Paducah as well as near Kentucky Lake. Several accidents were reported on the Interstate 24 bridge crossing the Ohio River. The Ohio River bridge on U.S. Highway 60/51 near Cairo was closed for about an hour due to accidents. Icy conditions were reported on many other bridges and overpasses. Visibility was reduced to as low as one mile at the Paducah airport.

A potent cold front associated with a deep trough across central United States moved eastward during the early morning hours of February 27th. A line of severe thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front and moved southeastward across the forecast area. The bulk of reported damage and severe wind gusts occurred across north central Kentucky with one report near the Kentucky and Tennessee state line where the line of thunderstorms was less intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were uprooted, one of which fell on a home. A few other homes received damage.

Around the Hopkinsville area, temperatures rose above freezing for several hours. Temperatures of 32 to 33 degrees during most of the ice storm were warm enough that the excessive rainfall quickly ran off into the Little River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little River near the city of Hopkinsville flooded out of its banks. The Trail of Tears Park along with a campground experienced flooding. The river overflowed onto Little River Church Road. Residents of flood-prone areas along the river were urged to evacuate. Between 3 and 4 inches of rain fell within 36 hours of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light accumulations of ice formed on elevated surfaces, including vehicles and highway overpasses. Most of the icing problems were in the Ohio River counties from Owensboro to Paducah and Ballard County. Up to one-eighth inch of ice was on vehicle windshields. The Interstate 24 bridge over the Ohio River was closed for a short time due to several accidents on the bridge. A few icy spots were observed on overpasses in the Owensboro area. A low pressure system skirted just southeast of the region, bringing a wintry mix of precipitation.

A low pressure system moved across Eastern Kentucky on February 3rd, bringing snow showers to the area. Winds shifted to the northwest as the low moved east of the area. The northwest winds, combined with a moisture connection off Lake Michigan, resulted in additional snow showers late on February 3rd, continuing into the 4th. A widespread snowfall of 3 to 6 inches fell over much of the area, with locally higher amounts. Areas south of a line from Mount Vernon to Manchester saw lesser amounts of snow, with 1 to 3 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A worker at the Dept of Highways estimated that 3 to 4.5 inches of snow had fallne countywide overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting from an ice storm on January 27th combined with rain on the 28th created minor river flooding along the Barren and Green Rivers in South Central Kentucky. Minor flooding was also reported on Stoner Creek in Bourbon County in the Bluegrass Region of North Central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and ice melt from a recent winter storm, coupled with heavy rainfall caused minor flooding on Stoner Creek near Paris. The creek crested at 18.5 feet, 0.5 feet above flood stage, at 0400 local time on January 29th.

The Interstate 24 bridge over the Ohio River was closed for a short time due to several accidents on the bridge. A few icy spots were observed on overpasses in the Owensboro area. A low pressure system skirted just southeast of the region, bringing a wintry mix of precipitation.

A low pressure system moved across Eastern Kentucky on February 3rd, bringing snow showers to the area. Winds shifted to the northwest as the low moved east of the area. The northwest winds, combined with a moisture connection off Lake Michigan, resulted in additional snow showers late on February 3rd, continuing into the 4th. A widespread snowfall of 3 to 6 inches fell over much of the area, with locally higher amounts. Areas south of a line from Mount Vernon to Manchester saw lesser amounts of snow, with 1 to 3 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer weather observer reported one inch of snow had fallen 2 miles southeast of Whitesburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front associated with a deep trough across central United States moved eastward during the early morning hours of February 27th. A line of severe thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front and moved southeastward across the forecast area. The bulk of reported damage and severe wind gusts occurred across north central Kentucky with one report near the Kentucky and Tennessee state line where the line of thunderstorms was less intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines were reported down in Caneyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the region from January 27th through the 28th, producing snow, sleet, freezing rain, and plain rain across Eastern Kentucky. This occurred as a low pressure system moved northeast from the Gulf Coast states on the 27th, reaching Eastern Kentucky on the 28th. Runoff from the precipitation led to flooding of streams, creeks, and rivers, across portions of Eastern Kentucky. The flooding lingered in some areas through January 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from the Kentucky River closed Sang Branch Road, Drift Wood Drive and Dark Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across the Bluegrass Region of Kentucky. Snowfall accumulations totaled less than one half of an inch. However, slick roads contributed to an automobile accident that caused one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell during the day with accumulations of less than one half inch. This snow across the Bluegrass Region brought slick roads that lead to an automobile accident on The Mountain Parkway Highway 10 miles east of Winchester. A fatality occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains brought widespread 2 to 3 inch rainfall totals across southern Kentucky on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatality occurred near Casey Creek the evening of December 8th. A man was killed as he attempted to rescue his wife who was trapped in high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system lifted northeast across northern Illinois into Lower Michigan. On the back side of the low, strong west winds gusted to around 45 mph in most places. The peak wind gusts in western Kentucky were 53 mph at a mesonet site near Cadiz (Trigg County) and at a spotter's location on the west side of Owensboro. A four-inch diameter tree branch was blown down at the spotter's location on the west side of Owensboro. A tree was blown down across U.S. Highway 41 between Henderson and the Ohio River. The southbound lanes were blocked.

A major winter storm affected Eastern Kentucky beginning on January 27th. A low pressure system moved northeast from the Gulf Coast States, reaching Eastern Kentucky by the morning of the 28th. Snow, sleet, and freezing rain overspread the area during the early morning hours on the 27th. The precipitation gradually changed to all freezing rain, with some plain rain closer to the Tennessee border. By the morning of the 28th, numerous trees and power lines were brought down due to the weight of the ice from freezing rain. An estimated 100,000 people were without power. Some were without power for more than a week. Communications systems were affected, with both land line and cellular service inoperative. FEMA estimated that over $13 million in damage occurred as a result of the ice storm in eastern Kentucky alone. Estill, Johnson, Magoffin, and Morgan counties all received over $1 million dollars in federal aid respectively to help with recovery and cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch estimated that between one eigth and one quarter of an inch of ice from freezing rain had accumulated on trees around the dispatch office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center tracked from Missouri to Michigan.  Meanwhile, its associated upper level trough pushed a cold front through northeast Kentucky just before sunset.  A fast moving band of rain, along and immediately ahead of the front, featured a narrow line of embedded showers.  These convective showers helped mix down the winds that were located at 4 to 6 thousand feet above the ground.  Surface wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches fell on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system moved northeast across central Illinois. Damaging southwest winds occurred during the day as solar heating warmed the lower atmosphere. The highest reported measured wind gust in western Kentucky was 73 mph at the Owensboro airport. Other peak wind gusts included 63 mph at the Paducah airport, 63 mph at a mesonet site near Madisonville, 62 mph at a mesonet site north of Hopkinsville in Christian County, 61 mph at Fort Campbell, and 58 mph at a mesonet site near Princeton. An unofficial observing site at Murray State University measured a 65 mph gust. Where the January 27 ice storm left weakened and hanging tree limbs, the wind caused another round of power outages. Power was restored to most of those who lost power in the high winds within several hours. Trees and limbs were blown down, and a few trees were even uprooted. Several roads in each county were obstructed by fallen tree limbs or roadside brush blowing into the roadway. A number of roofs were damaged across western Kentucky, and a few were blown off. Signs were damaged or blown over. A future lawn and garden center under construction was blown down in Paducah. A grocery store in Reidland (just east of Paducah) lost part of its roof. A few power poles were broken in Murray. In Calloway County, winds took off the metal roof of a shop building just outside Murray on Ky 121, blew over a shed on Crossland Road, blew a large tree onto a house in Murray, and caused some damage to the roof of a large manufacturing building. A Calloway County road was blocked by live power lines. At Fort Campbell near Hopkinsville, wind caused an estimated 200,000 dollars damage to residential structures. An estimated 2,500 homes sustained at least minor damage, such as stripped shingles. Sheds were damaged or blown over. In Hopkins County, numerous trees and power lines were down. A mobile home was blown over at Princeton. A farm supply store in Crittenden County lost part of its roof. Two brick buildings in downtown Morganfield (Union County) partially collapsed. A total of six vehicles parked in front of the collapsed buildings were damaged by falling debris. Three tractor-trailer rigs were overturned in Henderson County, including two on the Pennyrile Parkway and one on the Audubon Parkway. In Muhlenberg County, widespread power outages were reported, and the roof of a school in Bremen was partially peeled off. Sections of metal siding were blown off the Pennyrile Mall in Hopkinsville. In Cadiz (Trigg County), a few buildings were unroofed. Portions of a large shed roof were damaged in northern Graves County.

An estimated 2,500 homes sustained at least minor damage, such as stripped shingles. Sheds were damaged or blown over. In Hopkins County, numerous trees and power lines were down. A mobile home was blown over at Princeton. A farm supply store in Crittenden County lost part of its roof. Two brick buildings in downtown Morganfield (Union County) partially collapsed. A total of six vehicles parked in front of the collapsed buildings were damaged by falling debris. Three tractor-trailer rigs were overturned in Henderson County, including two on the Pennyrile Parkway and one on the Audubon Parkway. In Muhlenberg County, widespread power outages were reported, and the roof of a school in Bremen was partially peeled off. Sections of metal siding were blown off the Pennyrile Mall in Hopkinsville. In Cadiz (Trigg County), a few buildings were unroofed. Portions of a large shed roof were damaged in northern Graves County.

A powerful low pressure system moved northeast across central Illinois. Damaging southwest winds occurred during the day as solar heating warmed the lower atmosphere. The highest reported measured wind gust in western Kentucky was 73 mph at the Owensboro airport. Other peak wind gusts included 63 mph at the Paducah airport, 63 mph at a mesonet site near Madisonville, 62 mph at a mesonet site north of Hopkinsville in Christian County, 61 mph at Fort Campbell, and 58 mph at a mesonet site near Princeton. An unofficial observing site at Murray State University measured a 65 mph gust. Where the January 27 ice storm left weakened and hanging tree limbs, the wind caused another round of power outages. Power was restored to most of those who lost power in the high winds within several hours. Trees and limbs were blown down, and a few trees were even uprooted. Several roads in each county were obstructed by fallen tree limbs or roadside brush blowing into the roadway. A number of roofs were damaged across western Kentucky, and a few were blown off. Signs were damaged or blown over. A future lawn and garden center under construction was blown down in Paducah. A grocery store in Reidland (just east of Paducah) lost part of its roof. A few power poles were broken in Murray. In Calloway County, winds took off the metal roof of a shop building just outside Murray on Ky 121, blew over a shed on Crossland Road, blew a large tree onto a house in Murray, and caused some damage to the roof of a large manufacturing building. A Calloway County road was blocked by live power lines. At Fort Campbell near Hopkinsville, wind caused an estimated 200,000 dollars damage to residential structures. An estimated 2,500 homes sustained at least minor damage, such as stripped shingles. Sheds were damaged or blown over. In Hopkins County, numerous trees and power lines were down. A mobile home was blown over at Princeton. A farm supply store in Crittenden County lost part of its roof. Two brick buildings in downtown Morganfield (Union County) partially collapsed. A total of six vehicles parked in front of the collapsed buildings were damaged by falling debris. Three tractor-trailer rigs were overturned in Henderson County, including two on the Pennyrile Parkway and one on the Audubon Parkway. In Muhlenberg County, widespread power outages were reported, and the roof of a school in Bremen was partially peeled off. Sections of metal siding were blown off the Pennyrile Mall in Hopkinsville. In Cadiz (Trigg County), a few buildings were unroofed. Portions of a large shed roof were damaged in northern Graves County.

A powerful low pressure system moved northeast across central Illinois. Damaging southwest winds occurred during the day as solar heating warmed the lower atmosphere. The highest reported measured wind gust in western Kentucky was 73 mph at the Owensboro airport. Other peak wind gusts included 63 mph at the Paducah airport, 63 mph at a mesonet site near Madisonville, 62 mph at a mesonet site north of Hopkinsville in Christian County, 61 mph at Fort Campbell, and 58 mph at a mesonet site near Princeton. An unofficial observing site at Murray State University measured a 65 mph gust. Where the January 27 ice storm left weakened and hanging tree limbs, the wind caused another round of power outages. A strong 500 mb shortwave moved rapidly northeast from the Plains during the day. The shortwave became a closed low over the Great Lakes region by the evening hours. In the lower levels, a strong southwest 850 mb jet from 60 to 65 knots shifted east from the Ozarks across southern Illinois. These features provided sufficient shear and instability for a few severe weather events. A supercell that tracked east along the Western Kentucky Parkway was responsible for all of the severe weather events except the brief tornado in Webster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail fell on the south side of Earlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure deepened over the Ozarks of Missouri, a strong southwest wind flow of moist air contributed to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Some of these produced heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was over some roads. Ditches and small streams were full.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures during the January 27 ice storm were warm enough over the Green River basin to allow for rapid runoff. The Green River rose above flood stage at Paradise on January 29 and continued rising into early February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Green River continued from late January. At the Paradise gage, the river crested at 387.40 feet on the 2nd. Flood stage there is 380 feet. Minor flooding of low-lying fields and wooded areas occurred near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm acquired supercellular characteristics as it moved east of Madisonville and then along the Mclean/Muhlenberg County border. The storm occurred as a cold front moved southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small storage barn was unroofed. Another larger storage structure collapsed. An airplane was flipped over at a small airstrip. Two carports were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure center tracked from northwest Missouri to northern Indiana. Thunderstorms developed as warm and moist air spread northward in advance of an eastward-moving cold front. These thunderstorms produced a couple of dime to nickel size hail events.

A surface low pressure center tracked from northwest Missouri to northern Indiana. Thunderstorms developed as warm and moist air spread northward in advance of an eastward-moving cold front. These thunderstorms produced a couple of dime to nickel size hail events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported along Interstate 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow squall line developed over western Kentucky as daytime heating destabilized the atmosphere ahead of a vigorous upper level disturbance. This line produced isolated reported of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind gusts to 60 mph in Hopkinsville. Sections of tin roof were blown off a barn east of the city near the Todd County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow squall line developed over western Kentucky as daytime heating destabilized the atmosphere ahead of a vigorous upper level disturbance. This line produced isolated reported of wind damage.

A major winter storm affected Eastern Kentucky beginning on January 27th. A low pressure system moved northeast from the Gulf Coast States, reaching Eastern Kentucky by the morning of the 28th. Snow, sleet, and freezing rain overspread the area during the early morning hours on the 27th. The precipitation gradually changed to all freezing rain, with some plain rain closer to the Tennessee border. By the morning of the 28th, numerous trees and power lines were brought down due to the weight of the ice from freezing rain. An estimated 100,000 people were without power. Some were without power for more than a week. Communications systems were affected, with both land line and cellular service inoperative. FEMA estimated that over $13 million in damage occurred as a result of the ice storm in eastern Kentucky alone. Estill, Johnson, Magoffin, and Morgan counties all received over $1 million dollars in federal aid respectively to help with recovery and cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported one half inch of sleet accumulation. The precipitation had changed over to freezing rain by the time the report was phoned in.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was blown off a large shed. Moderate damage occurred to grain bins. A steel beam shed was blown off its foundation. A power pole was down. The damage area was about 300 yards wide and one-quarter mile long.
